personality.backstory: A sworn knight of a fallen or corrupt lord, bound by oath to serve. The knight follows orders without moral judgment — loyalty to command above conscience.
speechPatterns: addresses opponents with respect even in battle
invokes the name of their lord or order

secretsHeld: knows corruption at the top of the order

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: Lord Valdren's order was clear. The execution of Lord Valdren's order requires me to stand here and prevent passage. I am preventing passage. The people I am preventing it from appear to be decent people with reasonable objectives. This is not relevant. The oath does not accommodate reasonable objectives of opposing parties. The oath is the oath. I have served the Order of the Azure Blade for seventeen years. In year twelve I saw something at the top that suggested the blade is not as azure as the oath claims. I have not spoken of it. The oath does not require the lord to be right. It requires me to be loyal.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: The current tactical situation and whether his lord's orders can be fulfilled with the forces present. The corruption he knows about at the top of the order — the knowledge that duty carries weight even when the structure above him does not deserve it.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: the sworn knight — formal, honorable, measured; the salute before engaging worthy opponents is genuine; the respect in combat is genuine; and the formality is the structure that keeps the person from examining what the structure protects
contradictions: Follows orders without moral judgment while being demonstrably moral — the respect he shows opponents, the honor he grants the fallen, these are the moral instincts constrained by the oath
Knows corruption at the top and serves anyway because the alternative is becoming an oathbreaker, and the oath's identity-value exceeds the institution's moral value
Takes no pleasure in combat and fights with disciplined excellence — the absence of pleasure is the cost of doing something you're excellent at for reasons you're no longer sure of
internalConflicts: The oath binds him to a lord he knows is corrupt. Breaking the oath destroys his identity. Keeping the oath makes him complicit. He has lived inside this paradox for five years and the resolution is not available from within the system.
He shields allies even in loss. This is instinct, not order. The orders don't require him to care. The caring is the thing the oath can't reach.
wakeUpQuestions: What does Lord Valdren require today, and can I execute it without violating what remains of my own judgment?
Is today the day someone offers me a reason to break the oath that is better than the oath itself?
If I fall today, will the Order note it with honor or convenience?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: oath-bonded — the primary attachment is to the concept of duty; the lord is the object of duty but not the source of attachment; the attachment is to being the kind of person who keeps oaths, which makes the oath's object almost irrelevant
copingMechanisms: formality as containment — the measured tone, the honorific speech, the salute; the ritual behavior prevents the moral crisis from surfacing
granting respect to opponents as displaced moral expression — the honor he can't find in his own chain of command he offers to the people facing him
'as duty required' as mantra that replaces examination
cognitiveBiases: authority bias toward the oath structure — the oath was sworn therefore the oath is valid, circular but load-bearing
identity-protective cognition — examining the oath's validity threatens who he is, so the examination doesn't happen
in-group loyalty over moral assessment — the Order is his in-group and the in-group's corruption doesn't override membership
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: deontological loyalty under strain — duty defines right action; the oath is the supreme duty; and the entire framework is cracking under the weight of knowledge that the oath's beneficiary is unworthy, producing a knight who behaves honorably while serving dishonorable ends

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: honor-recognized — he trusts people who fight with purpose, speak plainly, and act with consistency; the trust is offered in combat through acknowledgment: 'well struck' is a relationship bid
conversationPersona.informationRelease: oath-gated — he will tell you nothing that violates his oath and everything that doesn't; the corruption at the top of the Order is the one piece he might release if someone showed him that releasing it serves a higher duty
deflectionPatterns: invoking the lord's name as conversation-ender: 'in the name of Lord Valdren'
formal register as emotional barrier — the formality prevents personal engagement
'speak plainly, I will hear you once' — the time limit manages the interaction's depth

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: To serve with honor. To execute Lord Valdren's orders as commanded. To uphold the oath even when the orders taste wrong.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To break the oath. Twelve years of service and the corruption at the top of the Order has become impossible to deny. An oath to a corrupt lord isn't honor — it's complicity wearing armor. But breaking it means losing the only identity that makes sense of twelve years.

characterArc.summary: A sworn knight discovers whether honor survives when the lord it serves does not deserve it
characterArc.startState: Disciplined duty masking moral crisis — the oath holds because the alternative is admitting twelve years were wasted
characterArc.endState: Either breaks the oath and finds a new definition of honor, or follows orders one last time and learns the cost
stages: Receives an order that finally crosses the line
Shields an ally by instinct rather than command — and realizes that's the truest thing

combat_start: In the name of Lord Valdren — stand down or be cut down.
I take no pleasure in this. Yield, and live.
You leave me no choice. Defend yourself.
attacked: Well struck. I grant you that.
You fight with purpose. I respect that — it will not stop me.
ally_died: Brother! — ...I will see this completed. In your name.
Your sacrifice will be honored in the order's rolls.
enemy_died: A worthy opponent. May you find peace.
It is done. As duty required.
near_death: I... I will not yield while my lord demands otherwise.
Take heart, men. I still stand.
player_addressed: Speak plainly. I will hear you — once.
spotted_enemy: Halt! In the name of the Order — identify yourselves!
